    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 485
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/485](https://www.erdosproblems.com/485)
    23→
    24→Let $f(k)$ be the minimum number of nonzero terms in $P(x)^2$, where $P \in \mathbb{Q}[x]$
    25→ranges over all polynomials with exactly $k$ nonzero terms. Is it true that
    26→$f(k) \to \infty$ as $k \to \infty$?
    27→
    28→[Re47] Rényi, A., _On the minimal number of terms of the square of a polynomial_,
    29→Hungarica Acta Math. (1947), 30–34.
    30→
    31→[Er49b] Erdős, P., _On the number of terms of the square of a polynomial_,
    32→Nieuw Arch. Wiskunde (2) (1949), 63–65.
    33→
    34→[Ha74] Hayman, W. K., _Research problems in function theory: new problems_, (1974), 155–180.
    35→
    36→[Sc87] Schinzel, A., _On the number of terms of a power of a polynomial_, Acta Arith. 49
    37→(1987), 55–70.
    38→
    39→[ScZa09] Schinzel, A. and Zannier, U., _On the number of terms of a power of a polynomial_,
    40→Atti Accad. Naz. Lincei Rend. Lincei Mat. Appl. 20 (2009), 95–98.
    41→-/
    42→
    43→open Polynomial
    44→
    45→namespace Erdos485
    46→
    47→/--
    48→Erdős Problem #485 (Erdős–Rényi conjecture, proved by Schinzel [Sc87]):
    49→
    50→Let $f(k)$ be the minimum number of nonzero terms in $P(x)^2$, where $P \in \mathbb{Q}[x]$
    51→ranges over all polynomials with exactly $k$ nonzero terms. Is it true that
    52→$f(k) \to \infty$ as $k \to \infty$?
    53→
    54→This is Problem 4.4 in [Ha74], where it is attributed to Erdős. First investigated
    55→by Rényi and Rédei [Re47]. Erdős [Er49b] proved the upper bound $f(k) < k^{1-c}$
    56→for some $c > 0$.
    57→
    58→Schinzel [Sc87] proved $f(k) > \log(\log k) / \log 2$. Schinzel and Zannier [ScZa09]
    59→improved this to $f(k) \gg \log k$.
    60→-/
    61→@[category research solved, AMS 12]
    62→theorem erdos_485 : answer(True) ↔
    63→    ∀ M : ℕ, ∃ K : ℕ, ∀ k : ℕ, K ≤ k →
    64→    ∀ P : ℚ[X], P.support.card = k →
    65→    M ≤ (P ^ 2).support.card := by
    66→  sorry
    67→
    68→/--
    69→Generalization of Erdős Problem #485 to arbitrary powers:
    70→
    71→For any fixed $n \geq 1$, the minimum number of nonzero terms of $P(x)^n$,
    72→over all polynomials $P \in \mathbb{Q}[x]$ with exactly $k$ nonzero terms,
    73→tends to infinity as $k \to \infty$.
    74→
    75→Schinzel [Sc87] established lower bounds for this general case over fields
    76→of characteristic zero or sufficiently large positive characteristic.
    77→-/
    78→@[category research solved, AMS 12]
    79→theorem erdos_485_general_power :
    80→    ∀ (n : ℕ), 1 ≤ n →
    81→    ∀ M : ℕ, ∃ K : ℕ, ∀ k : ℕ, K ≤ k →
    82→    ∀ P : ℚ[X], P.support.card = k →
    83→    M ≤ (P ^ n).support.card := by
    84→  sorry
    85→
    86→end Erdos485
    87→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 487
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/487](https://www.erdosproblems.com/487)
    23→
    24→If $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ has positive upper density, must there exist distinct
    25→$a, b, c \in A$ such that $\text{lcm}(a, b) = c$?
    26→
    27→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Graph theory and probability. II_. Canad. J. Math. 13 (1961), p. 236.
    28→
    29→[Er65b] Erdős, P., _Extremal problems in number theory_. Proc. Sympos. Pure Math. 8 (1965),
    30→p. 228.
    31→
    32→[Kl71] Kleitman, D., _Collections of subsets containing no two sets and their union_.
    33→Proc. LA Meeting AMS (1971), 153-155.
    34→
    35→[DaEr36] Davenport, H. and Erdős, P., _On sequences of positive integers_.
    36→Acta Arithmetica **2** (1936), 147-151.
    37→-/
    38→
    39→namespace Erdos487
    40→
    41→/--
    42→Erdős Problem 487 [Er61, p. 236] [Er65b, p. 228]:
    43→
    44→Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ have positive upper density. Must there exist distinct
    45→$a, b, c \in A$ such that $[a, b] = c$ (where $[a, b]$ denotes the least common multiple of
    46→$a$ and $b$)?
    47→
    48→This is true, a consequence of the positive solution to Erdős Problem 447
    49→by Kleitman [Kl71].
    50→-/
    51→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    52→theorem erdos_487 : answer(True) ↔
    53→    ∀ (A : Set ℕ), A.upperDensity > 0 →
    54→    ∃ a ∈ A, ∃ b ∈ A, ∃ c ∈ A, a ≠ b ∧ a ≠ c ∧ b ≠ c ∧ Nat.lcm a b = c := by
    55→  sorry
    56→
    57→end Erdos487
    58→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 490
    21→
    22→If $A, B \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$ are such that all products $ab$ ($a \in A$, $b \in B$)
    23→are distinct, is it true that $|A| \cdot |B| \ll N^2 / \log N$?
    24→
    25→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/490](https://www.erdosproblems.com/490)
    26→
    27→See also Problems 425 and 896.
    28→
    29→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems_. Magyar Tud. Akad. Mat. Kutató Int. Közl.
    30→**6** (1961), 221–254.
    31→
    32→[Er69] Erdős, P., _On some applications of graph theory to number theoretic problems_. Publ.
    33→Ramanujan Inst. 1 (1969), 131-136.
    34→
    35→[Er72] Erdős, P., _Quelques problèmes de théorie des nombres_, p. 81, 1972.
    36→
    37→[Er73] Erdős, P., _Problems and results on combinatorial number theory_. In: A Survey of
    38→Combinatorial Theory (1973), 117-138.
    39→
    40→[Sz76] Szemerédi, E., _On a problem of P. Erdős_. J. Number Theory (1976), 264-270.
    41→-/
    42→
    43→namespace Erdos490
    44→
    45→/--
    46→The products $ab$ with $a \in A$ and $b \in B$ are all distinct: the multiplication
    47→map $A \times B \to \mathbb{N}$ is injective.
    48→-/
    49→def HasDistinctProducts (A B : Finset ℕ) : Prop :=
    50→  ∀ a₁ ∈ A, ∀ b₁ ∈ B, ∀ a₂ ∈ A, ∀ b₂ ∈ B,
    51→    a₁ * b₁ = a₂ * b₂ → a₁ = a₂ ∧ b₁ = b₂
    52→
    53→/--
    54→If $A, B \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$ are such that all products $ab$ ($a \in A$, $b \in B$)
    55→are distinct, then $|A| \cdot |B| \ll N^2 / \log N$.
    56→
    57→Formally: there exists a constant $C > 0$ such that for all $N \geq 2$ and all
    58→$A, B \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$ with distinct products,
    59→$$
    60→  |A| \cdot |B| \le C \cdot \frac{N^2}{\log N}.
    61→$$
    62→
    63→Proved by Szemerédi [Sz76].
    64→-/
    65→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    66→theorem erdos_490 :
    67→    ∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧
    68→      ∀ (N : ℕ), 2 ≤ N →
    69→        ∀ (A B : Finset ℕ),
    70→        (∀ a ∈ A, 1 ≤ a ∧ a ≤ N) →
    71→        (∀ b ∈ B, 1 ≤ b ∧ b ≤ N) →
    72→        HasDistinctProducts A B →
    73→        (A.card : ℝ) * (B.card : ℝ) ≤ C * ((N : ℝ) ^ 2 / Real.log (N : ℝ)) := by
    74→  sorry
    75→
    76→/--
    77→Does the limit $\lim_{N \to \infty} \max_{A,B \subseteq [N]} |A| |B| \log N / N^2$ exist,
    78→where the maximum is over $A, B$ with distinct products? If it exists, it must be $\geq 1$.
    79→
    80→This variant captures the question of whether the constant in Szemerédi's bound is sharp
    81→and whether the limit exists at all. Posed by Erdős [Er72].
    82→-/
    83→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    84→theorem erdos_490_limit :
    85→    ∃ L : ℝ, L ≥ 1 ∧
    86→      Filter.Tendsto
    87→        (fun N : ℕ =>
    88→          sSup {(A.card : ℝ) * (B.card : ℝ) * Real.log (N : ℝ) / ((N : ℝ) ^ 2) |
    89→            (A : Finset ℕ) (B : Finset ℕ)
    90→            (_ : ∀ a ∈ A, 1 ≤ a ∧ a ≤ N)
    91→            (_ : ∀ b ∈ B, 1 ≤ b ∧ b ≤ N)
    92→            (_ : HasDistinctProducts A B)})
    93→        Filter.atTop (nhds L) := by
    94→  sorry
    95→
    96→end Erdos490
    97→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 491
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/491](https://www.erdosproblems.com/491)
    23→
    24→If $f$ is an additive arithmetic function (i.e. $f(ab) = f(a) + f(b)$ whenever $\gcd(a,b) = 1$)
    25→and there is a constant $c$ such that $|f(n+1) - f(n)| < c$ for all $n$, must there exist a
    26→constant $c'$ such that $f(n) = c' \log n + O(1)$?
    27→
    28→See also Problems 897 and 1122.
    29→
    30→[Er46] Erdős, P., _On the distribution function of additive functions_. Ann. of Math. (2) 47
    31→(1946), 1-20.
    32→
    33→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems_. Magyar Tud. Akad. Mat. Kutató Int. Közl. **6**
    34→(1961), 221–254.
    35→
    36→[Er72] Erdős, P., _Quelques problèmes de théorie des nombres_, p. 81, 1972.
    37→
    38→[Er82e] Erdős, P., _Some new problems and results in number theory_ (1982).
    39→
    40→[Wi70] Wirsing, E., _A characterization of $\log n$ as an additive arithmetic function_.
    41→Symposia Math. (1970), 45–47.
    42→-/
    43→
    44→namespace Erdos491
    45→
    46→/--
    47→An additive arithmetic function: $f(ab) = f(a) + f(b)$ whenever $\gcd(a,b) = 1$.
    48→-/
    49→def IsAdditiveArithmeticFunction (f : ℕ → ℝ) : Prop :=
    50→  ∀ a b : ℕ, Nat.Coprime a b → f (a * b) = f a + f b
    51→
    52→/--
    53→Erdős Problem 491 (proved by Wirsing [Wi70]):
    54→
    55→Let $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$ be an additive function (i.e. $f(ab) = f(a) + f(b)$ whenever
    56→$\gcd(a,b) = 1$). If there is a constant $c$ such that $|f(n+1) - f(n)| < c$ for
    57→all $n$, then there exist constants $c'$ and $M$ such that $|f(n) - c' \log n| \leq M$
    58→for all $n \geq 1$.
    59→-/
    60→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    61→theorem erdos_491 : answer(True) ↔
    62→    ∀ f : ℕ → ℝ, IsAdditiveArithmeticFunction f →
    63→    (∃ c : ℝ, ∀ n : ℕ, |f (n + 1) - f n| < c) →
    64→    ∃ c' : ℝ, ∃ M : ℝ, ∀ n : ℕ, 1 ≤ n →
    65→      |f n - c' * Real.log (n : ℝ)| ≤ M := by
    66→  sorry
    67→
    68→/--
    69→Erdős (1946) [Er46]: If $f$ is an additive function and $f(n+1) - f(n) = o(1)$
    70→(i.e., the differences tend to zero), then $f(n) = c \log n$ for some constant $c$
    71→(exact equality, not just up to $O(1)$). This is a stronger hypothesis giving a
    72→stronger conclusion than the main problem.
    73→-/
    74→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    75→theorem erdos_491_small_o : ∀ f : ℕ → ℝ, IsAdditiveArithmeticFunction f →
    76→    (∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε → ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N ≤ n → |f (n + 1) - f n| < ε) →
    77→    ∃ c : ℝ, ∀ n : ℕ, 1 ≤ n → f n = c * Real.log (n : ℝ) := by
    78→  sorry
    79→
    80→/--
    81→Erdős (1946) [Er46]: If $f$ is an additive function and $f$ is monotone non-decreasing
    82→(i.e., $f(n+1) \geq f(n)$ for all $n$), then $f(n) = c \log n$ for some constant $c$.
    83→-/
    84→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    85→theorem erdos_491_monotone : ∀ f : ℕ → ℝ, IsAdditiveArithmeticFunction f →
    86→    (∀ n : ℕ, f n ≤ f (n + 1)) →
    87→    ∃ c : ℝ, ∀ n : ℕ, 1 ≤ n → f n = c * Real.log (n : ℝ) := by
    88→  sorry
    89→
    90→end Erdos491
    91→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 492
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/492](https://www.erdosproblems.com/492)
    23→
    24→Let $A = \{a_1 < a_2 < \cdots\} \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be infinite with $a_{i+1}/a_i \to 1$.
    25→The problem asked whether, for almost all $\alpha > 0$, the sequence of fractional positions
    26→$f(\alpha n)$ is uniformly distributed in $[0,1)$. Disproved by Schmidt.
    27→
    28→[LV53] Le Veque, W.J., _On uniform distribution modulo a subdivision_,
    29→  Pacific J. Math. (1953), 757-771.
    30→[DaLe63] Davenport, H., LeVeque, W.J., _Uniform distribution relative to a fixed sequence_,
    31→  Michigan Math. J. (1963), 315-319.
    32→[DaEr63] Davenport, H., Erdős, P., _A theorem on uniform distribution_,
    33→  Magyar Tud. Akad. Mat. Kutató Int. Közl. (1963), 3-11.
    34→[Sc69] Schmidt, W.M., _Irregularities of distribution. IV_, Invent. Math. 7 (1969), 55-82.
    35→-/
    36→
    37→open Filter MeasureTheory Finset
    38→
    39→namespace Erdos492
    40→
    41→attribute [local instance] Classical.propDecidable
    42→
    43→/--
    44→A sequence $x : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$ is equidistributed in $[0,1)$ if for every subinterval
    45→$[c, d) \subseteq [0,1)$, the proportion of indices $n < N$ with $x(n) \in [c, d)$
    46→converges to $d - c$ as $N \to \infty$.
    47→-/
    48→def IsEquidistributed (x : ℕ → ℝ) : Prop :=
    49→  ∀ c d : ℝ, 0 ≤ c → c < d → d ≤ 1 →
    50→    Tendsto (fun N : ℕ =>
    51→      (((Finset.range N).filter (fun n => c ≤ x n ∧ x n < d)).card : ℝ) / (N : ℝ))
    52→      atTop (nhds (d - c))
    53→
    54→/--
    55→Given a strictly increasing sequence $a : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$, for $x$ in some interval
    56→$[a(i), a(i+1))$, returns the fractional position
    57→$(x - a(i)) / (a(i+1) - a(i)) \in [0,1)$.
    58→Returns $0$ if $x$ is not in any such interval.
    59→-/
    60→noncomputable def fractionalPosition (a : ℕ → ℕ) (x : ℝ) : ℝ :=
    61→  if h : ∃ i : ℕ, (a i : ℝ) ≤ x ∧ x < (a (i + 1) : ℝ) then
    62→    let i := Nat.find h
    63→    (x - (a i : ℝ)) / ((a (i + 1) : ℝ) - (a i : ℝ))
    64→  else 0
    65→
    66→/--
    67→Erdős Problem 492 (disproved by Schmidt [Sc69]):
    68→
    69→Let $A = \{a_1 < a_2 < \cdots\} \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be infinite with $a_{i+1}/a_i \to 1$.
    70→For $x \geq a_1$, define $f(x) = (x - a_i)/(a_{i+1} - a_i) \in [0,1)$
    71→where $a_i \leq x < a_{i+1}$. For example if $A = \mathbb{N}$ then $f(x) = \{x\}$ is
    72→the usual fractional part operator.
    73→
    74→The conjecture asked whether for almost all $\alpha > 0$, the sequence
    75→$f(\alpha n)$ is uniformly distributed in $[0,1)$. Schmidt showed this is false:
    76→there exists such a sequence for which equidistribution fails on a
    77→set of positive measure.
    78→-/
    79→@[category research solved, AMS 11 28]
    80→theorem erdos_492 : answer(False) ↔
    81→    ∀ (a : ℕ → ℕ), StrictMono a → (∀ i, 0 < a i) →
    82→      Tendsto (fun i => (a (i + 1) : ℝ) / (a i : ℝ)) atTop (nhds 1) →
    83→      ∀ᵐ α ∂(volume : Measure ℝ).restrict (Set.Ioi 0),
    84→        IsEquidistributed (fun n => fractionalPosition a (α * (n : ℝ))) := by
    85→  sorry
    86→
    87→/--
    88→Davenport–LeVeque (1963) [DaLe63]: If $a_{n+1} - a_n$ is monotone and $a_{n+1}/a_n \to 1$,
    89→then for almost all $\alpha > 0$, the sequence $f(\alpha n)$ is equidistributed in $[0,1)$.
    90→This is a positive partial result for Erdős Problem 492 under a monotone-differences hypothesis.
    91→-/
    92→@[category research solved, AMS 11 28]
    93→theorem erdos_492_monotone_differences :
    94→    ∀ (a : ℕ → ℕ), StrictMono a → (∀ i, 0 < a i) →
    95→      Tendsto (fun i => (a (i + 1) : ℝ) / (a i : ℝ)) atTop (nhds 1) →
    96→      Monotone (fun i => a (i + 1) - a i) →
    97→      ∀ᵐ α ∂(volume : Measure ℝ).restrict (Set.Ioi 0),
    98→        IsEquidistributed (fun n => fractionalPosition a (α * (n : ℝ))) := by
    99→  sorry
   100→
   101→/--
   102→Davenport–Erdős (1963) [DaEr63]: If $a_n \gg n^{1/2+\varepsilon}$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$
   103→(i.e., $a_n$ grows at least as fast as $n^{1/2+\varepsilon}$), then for almost all $\alpha > 0$,
   104→the sequence $f(\alpha n)$ is equidistributed in $[0,1)$.
   105→This is a positive partial result for Erdős Problem 492 under a growth-rate hypothesis.
   106→-/
   107→@[category research solved, AMS 11 28]
   108→theorem erdos_492_growth_rate :
   109→    ∀ (a : ℕ → ℕ), StrictMono a → (∀ i, 0 < a i) →
   110→      Tendsto (fun i => (a (i + 1) : ℝ) / (a i : ℝ)) atTop (nhds 1) →
   111→      (∃ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε ∧ ∃ C : ℝ, 0 < C ∧ ∀ n : ℕ, C * (n : ℝ) ^ (1/2 + ε) ≤ (a n : ℝ)) →
   112→      ∀ᵐ α ∂(volume : Measure ℝ).restrict (Set.Ioi 0),
   113→        IsEquidistributed (fun n => fractionalPosition a (α * (n : ℝ))) := by
   114→  sorry
   115→
   116→end Erdos492
   117→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 493
    21→
    22→Does there exist a $k$ such that every sufficiently large integer can be written
    23→in the form $\prod_i a_i - \sum_i a_i$ for some integers $a_i \geq 2$?
    24→
    25→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/493](https://www.erdosproblems.com/493)
    26→
    27→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems_. Magyar Tud. Akad. Mat. Kutató Int. Közl. 6 (1961),
    28→p. 238.
    29→-/
    30→
    31→open Finset BigOperators
    32→
    33→namespace Erdos493
    34→
    35→/--
    36→Erdős Problem 493 (attributed to Schinzel):
    37→Does there exist a $k$ such that every sufficiently large integer can be written
    38→in the form $\prod_i a_i - \sum_i a_i$ for some integers $a_i \geq 2$?
    39→
    40→The answer is yes with $k = 2$: for any integer $n$ we have
    41→$n = 2 \cdot (n+2) - (2 + (n+2))$ (observed by Eli Seamans).
    42→-/
    43→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    44→theorem erdos_493 : answer(True) ↔
    45→    ∃ k : ℕ, ∃ N : ℤ, ∀ n : ℤ, n ≥ N →
    46→      ∃ a : Fin k → ℤ, (∀ i, a i ≥ 2) ∧
    47→        (∏ i : Fin k, a i) - (∑ i : Fin k, a i) = n := by
    48→  sorry
    49→
    50→end Erdos493
    51→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 496
    21→
    22→Erdős asked whether for every irrational α and every ε > 0 there exist positive integers x, y, z
    23→with |x² + y² − z²α| < ε. An analogous result for quadratic forms in five variables was proved
    24→by Davenport and Heilbronn (1946). The full result was proved by Margulis (1989) as a consequence
    25→of the Oppenheim conjecture.
    26→
    27→## References
    28→
    29→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems_. Magyar Tud. Akad. Mat. Kutató Int. Közl. **6**
    30→(1961), 221–254.
    31→
    32→[DaHe46] Davenport, H., Heilbronn, H., _On indefinite quadratic forms in five variables_.
    33→J. London Math. Soc. (1946), 185–193.
    34→
    35→[Ma89] Margulis, G. A., _Discrete subgroups and ergodic theory_. Number theory, trace formulas
    36→and discrete groups (Oslo, 1987) (1989), 377–398.
    37→
    38→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/496](https://www.erdosproblems.com/496)
    39→-/
    40→
    41→namespace Erdos496
    42→
    43→/--
    44→Erdős Problem #496 (Oppenheim conjecture, proved by Margulis):
    45→Let $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$ be irrational and $\varepsilon > 0$. Then there exist positive integers
    46→$x$, $y$, $z$ such that $|x^2 + y^2 - z^2 \alpha| < \varepsilon$.
    47→-/
    48→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    49→theorem erdos_496 (α : ℝ) (hα : Irrational α) (ε : ℝ) (hε : ε > 0) :
    50→    ∃ x y z : ℕ, 0 < x ∧ 0 < y ∧ 0 < z ∧
    51→      |(x : ℝ) ^ 2 + (y : ℝ) ^ 2 - (z : ℝ) ^ 2 * α| < ε := by
    52→  sorry
    53→
    54→end Erdos496
    55→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 497
    21→
    22→How many antichains are there in the power set of an n-element set?
    23→
    24→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/497](https://www.erdosproblems.com/497)
    25→
    26→OEIS: [A000372](https://oeis.org/A000372)
    27→
    28→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems_. Magyar Tud. Akad. Mat. Kutató Int. Közl. 6 (1961),
    29→221–254.
    30→
    31→[Kl69] Kleitman, D., _On Dedekind's Problem: The Number of Monotone Boolean Functions_.
    32→Proc. Amer. Math. Soc. 21 (1969), 677–682.
    33→-/
    34→
    35→open Finset Filter
    36→
    37→namespace Erdos497
    38→
    39→open Classical in
    40→/--
    41→The number of antichains in the power set of $\operatorname{Fin} n$ (the Dedekind number $D(n)$).
    42→An antichain is a family $F$ of subsets of $[n]$ such that for all $A, B \in F$,
    43→$A \neq B$ implies $A \not\subset B$.
    44→-/
    45→noncomputable def dedekindNumber (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    46→  Fintype.card {F : Finset (Finset (Fin n)) // IsAntichain (· ⊆ ·) (F : Set (Finset (Fin n)))}
    47→
    48→/--
    49→Erdős Problem 497 (Dedekind's Problem, resolved by Kleitman [Kl69]):
    50→How many antichains in $[n]$ are there? That is, how many families of subsets
    51→of $[n]$ are there such that no member is a subset of another?
    52→
    53→Sperner's theorem states that any single antichain has size at most
    54→$\binom{n}{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor}$. Kleitman proved that the total number of antichains in the
    55→power set of $[n]$ is $2^{(1+o(1)) \binom{n}{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor}}$.
    56→
    57→Equivalently, $\log_2(D(n)) / \binom{n}{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} \to 1$ as $n \to \infty$.
    58→-/
    59→@[category research solved, AMS 5 6]
    60→theorem erdos_497 :
    61→    Tendsto
    62→      (fun n : ℕ => Real.log (dedekindNumber n : ℝ) / (Real.log 2 * (n.choose (n / 2) : ℝ)))
    63→      atTop (nhds 1) := by
    64→  sorry
    65→
    66→end Erdos497
    67→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 498
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/498](https://www.erdosproblems.com/498)
    23→
    24→Given complex numbers $z_1, \ldots, z_n$ with $|z_i| \geq 1$, the number of sign patterns
    25→$\varepsilon \in \{-1,1\}^n$ for which the signed sum $\sum \varepsilon_i z_i$ lands in any unit
    26→disc is at most $\binom{n}{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor}$.
    27→
    28→See also: Problem 395 (reverse Littlewood–Offord problem).
    29→
    30→[Er45] Erdős, P., _On a lemma of Littlewood and Offord_. Bull. Amer. Math. Soc. **51** (1945),
    31→898–902.
    32→
    33→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems_. Magyar Tud. Akad. Mat. Kutató Int. Közl. **6** (1961),
    34→221–254.
    35→
    36→[Kl65] Kleitman, D., _On a lemma of Littlewood and Offord on the distributions of linear
    37→combinations of vectors_. Advances in Math. **1** (1965), 155–157.
    38→
    39→[Kl70] Kleitman, D., _On a lemma of Littlewood and Offord on the distribution of certain sums_.
    40→Math. Z. **90** (1970), 251–259.
    41→-/
    42→
    43→open Finset
    44→open scoped BigOperators
    45→
    46→namespace Erdos498
    47→
    48→/-- The signed sum of complex numbers $z$ with signs $\varepsilon \in \{-1, 1\}^n$ (encoded as
    49→`Bool`). -/
    50→noncomputable def signedSum {n : ℕ} (z : Fin n → ℂ) (ε : Fin n → Bool) : ℂ :=
    51→  ∑ i : Fin n, (if ε i then (1 : ℂ) else (-1 : ℂ)) * z i
    52→
    53→/--
    54→Erdős Problem 498 (Littlewood–Offord problem, proved by Kleitman [Kl65]):
    55→
    56→Let $z_1, \ldots, z_n \in \mathbb{C}$ with $|z_i| \geq 1$ for all $i$. For any disc of radius $1$
    57→centered at $w \in \mathbb{C}$, the number of sign patterns $\varepsilon \in \{-1, 1\}^n$ such that
    58→$\varepsilon_1 z_1 + \cdots + \varepsilon_n z_n$ lies in the disc (i.e.,
    59→$\|\sum \varepsilon_i z_i - w\| \leq 1$) is at most $\binom{n}{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor}$.
    60→
    61→Erdős [Er45] proved the real case. Kleitman [Kl65] proved the full complex case and later
    62→generalised the result to arbitrary Hilbert spaces [Kl70].
    63→-/
    64→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    65→theorem erdos_498 (n : ℕ) (z : Fin n → ℂ) (w : ℂ)
    66→    (hz : ∀ i, 1 ≤ ‖z i‖) :
    67→    (Finset.univ.filter (fun ε : Fin n → Bool =>
    68→      ‖signedSum z ε - w‖ ≤ 1)).card ≤ n.choose (n / 2) := by
    69→  sorry
    70→
    71→end Erdos498
    72→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 500
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/500](https://www.erdosproblems.com/500)
    23→
    24→What is $\mathrm{ex}_3(n, K_4^3)$? That is, the largest number of $3$-edges which can be placed
    25→on $n$ vertices so that there exists no $K_4^3$, a set of $4$ vertices which is covered
    26→by all $4$ possible $3$-edges.
    27→
    28→A problem of Turán. Turán observed that dividing the vertices into three equal
    29→parts $X_1, X_2, X_3$, and taking the edges to be those triples that either have
    30→exactly one vertex in each part or two vertices in $X_i$ and one vertex in $X_{i+1}$
    31→(where $X_4 = X_1$) shows that
    32→$$
    33→  \mathrm{ex}_3(n, K_4^3) \geq (5/9 + o(1)) \binom{n}{3}.
    34→$$
    35→This is probably the truth. The current best upper bound is
    36→$$
    37→  \mathrm{ex}_3(n, K_4^3) \leq 0.5611666 \binom{n}{3},
    38→$$
    39→due to Razborov [Ra10].
    40→
    41→See also: Problem 712 (general case for r-uniform, k-clique).
    42→
    43→OEIS: [A140462](https://oeis.org/A140462)
    44→
    45→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Problems in combinatorics_ (1961).
    46→
    47→[Er71] Erdős, P., _Topics in combinatorial analysis_. Proc. Second Louisiana Conf. on
    48→Combinatorics, Graph Theory and Computing (1971), 2–20.
    49→
    50→[Er74c] Erdős, P., _Problems and results on combinatorial number theory III_ (1974).
    51→
    52→[Er81] Erdős, P., _Combinatorial problems and results_ (1981).
    53→
    54→[Ra10] Razborov, A., _On 3-hypergraphs with forbidden 4-vertex configurations_.
    55→SIAM J. Discrete Math. (2010), 946–963.
    56→-/
    57→
    58→open Finset Filter
    59→
    60→namespace Erdos500
    61→
    62→/-- A $3$-uniform hypergraph on `Fin n` is $K_4^3$-free if every edge has exactly $3$
    63→vertices and no $4$ vertices span all $4$ possible $3$-element subsets. -/
    64→def IsK43Free {n : ℕ} (H : Finset (Finset (Fin n))) : Prop :=
    65→  (∀ e ∈ H, e.card = 3) ∧
    66→  ∀ S : Finset (Fin n), S.card = 4 → ¬(S.powersetCard 3 ⊆ H)
    67→
    68→/-- The $3$-uniform Turán number $\mathrm{ex}_3(n, K_4^3)$: the maximum number of $3$-element
    69→subsets of an $n$-element set such that no $4$ vertices span all $4$ triples. -/
    70→noncomputable def ex3K43 (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    71→  sSup {m : ℕ | ∃ H : Finset (Finset (Fin n)), IsK43Free H ∧ H.card = m}
    72→
    73→/--
    74→Erdős Problem 500 — Turán's Hypergraph Conjecture [Er61][Er71][Er74c][Er81]:
    75→
    76→The $3$-uniform Turán density for $K_4^3$ is exactly $5/9$. Equivalently,
    77→$$
    78→  \mathrm{ex}_3(n, K_4^3) / \binom{n}{3} \to 5/9 \text{ as } n \to \infty.
    79→$$
    80→Turán conjectured that the lower bound construction (partition into $3$ equal parts,
    81→take triples with one vertex in each part or two in $X_i$ and one in $X_{i+1}$) is
    82→optimal. This remains open. The best known upper bound is $\leq 0.5611666 \binom{n}{3}$
    83→due to Razborov [Ra10].
    84→-/
    85→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    86→theorem erdos_500 :
    87→    Tendsto
    88→      (fun n : ℕ => (ex3K43 n : ℝ) / (Nat.choose n 3 : ℝ))
    89→      atTop (nhds answer(sorry)) := by
    90→  sorry
    91→
    92→end Erdos500
    93→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 501
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/501](https://www.erdosproblems.com/501)
    23→
    24→For every $x \in \mathbb{R}$, let $A_x \subset \mathbb{R}$ be a bounded set with outer measure
    25→$< 1$. Must there exist an infinite independent set, that is, some infinite $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$
    26→such that $x \notin A_y$ for all $x \neq y \in X$?
    27→
    28→If the sets $A_x$ are closed and have measure $< 1$, then must there exist an
    29→independent set of size $3$?
    30→
    31→Erdős and Hajnal [ErHa60] proved the existence of arbitrarily large finite independent
    32→sets (under the assumptions in the first problem). Gladysz [Gl62] proved the existence
    33→of an independent set of size 2 under the closed hypothesis. Hechler [He72] showed the
    34→answer to the first question is no, assuming the continuum hypothesis. Newelski,
    35→Pawlikowski, and Seredyński [NPS87] proved that if all the $A_x$ are closed with
    36→measure $< 1$ then there is an infinite independent set.
    37→
    38→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems_. Magyar Tud. Akad. Mat. Kutató Int. Közl.
    39→6 (1961), 221–254.
    40→
    41→[ErHa60] Erdős, P. and Hajnal, A., _Some remarks on set theory. VIII_.
    42→Michigan Math. J. 7 (1960), 187–191.
    43→
    44→[Gl62] Gladysz, S., _Bemerkungen über die Unabhängigkeit der Punkte in Bezug auf
    45→mengenwertige Funktionen_. Acta Math. Acad. Sci. Hungar. 13 (1962), 199–201.
    46→
    47→[He72] Hechler, S. H., _On two problems in combinatorial set theory_. Bull. Acad.
    48→Polon. Sci. Sér. Sci. Math. Astronom. Phys. 20 (1972), 429–431.
    49→
    50→[NPS87] Newelski, L., Pawlikowski, J., and Seredyński, W., _Infinite free set for
    51→small measure set mappings_. Proc. Amer. Math. Soc. 100 (1987), 335–339.
    52→-/
    53→
    54→open MeasureTheory Set
    55→
    56→-- In Mathlib, `volume s` computes the outer measure for any set `s`,
    57→-- whether or not `s` is measurable.
    58→
    59→namespace Erdos501
    60→
    61→/-- A set $X$ is independent with respect to a family of sets $A$ if for all
    62→distinct $x, y \in X$, we have $x \notin A(y)$. -/
    63→def IndependentFamily (A : ℝ → Set ℝ) (X : Set ℝ) : Prop :=
    64→  ∀ x ∈ X, ∀ y ∈ X, x ≠ y → x ∉ A y
    65→
    66→/--
    67→Erdős Problem 501 [Er61][ErHa60]: If each $A_x$ is bounded with outer measure $< 1$, must
    68→there exist an infinite independent set?
    69→
    70→Erdős and Hajnal [ErHa60] proved the existence of arbitrarily large finite independent
    71→sets. Hechler [He72] showed the answer is no assuming the continuum hypothesis. The full
    72→ZFC status remains open.
    73→-/
    74→@[category research open, AMS 3 28]
    75→theorem erdos_501 :
    76→    answer(sorry) ↔
    77→    ∀ A : ℝ → Set ℝ,
    78→      (∀ x : ℝ, Bornology.IsBounded (A x) ∧ volume (A x) < 1) →
    79→      ∃ X : Set ℝ, X.Infinite ∧ IndependentFamily A X := by
    80→  sorry
    81→
    82→/--
    83→Erdős Problem 501, closed variant [Er61][ErHa60]: If each $A_x$ is closed with
    84→measure $< 1$, must there exist an independent set of size $3$?
    85→
    86→Newelski, Pawlikowski, and Seredyński [NPS87] proved that under these hypotheses there
    87→is in fact an infinite independent set (see `erdos_501.variants.closed_measure_infinite`).
    88→-/
    89→@[category research solved, AMS 3 28]
    90→theorem erdos_501.variants.closed_measure :
    91→    answer(True) ↔
    92→    ∀ A : ℝ → Set ℝ,
    93→      (∀ x : ℝ, IsClosed (A x) ∧ volume (A x) < 1) →
    94→      ∃ x y z : ℝ, x ≠ y ∧ y ≠ z ∧ x ≠ z ∧
    95→        IndependentFamily A {x, y, z} := by
    96→  sorry
    97→
    98→/--
    99→Erdős Problem 501, closed variant — infinite independent set [NPS87]: If each $A_x$ is
   100→closed with measure $< 1$, then there exists an infinite independent set.
   101→
   102→This is the full strength of the result of Newelski, Pawlikowski, and Seredyński [NPS87],
   103→which is strictly stronger than the size-3 statement in `erdos_501.variants.closed_measure`.
   104→-/
   105→@[category research solved, AMS 3 28]
   106→theorem erdos_501.variants.closed_measure_infinite :
   107→    answer(True) ↔
   108→    ∀ A : ℝ → Set ℝ,
   109→      (∀ x : ℝ, IsClosed (A x) ∧ volume (A x) < 1) →
   110→      ∃ X : Set ℝ, X.Infinite ∧ IndependentFamily A X := by
   111→  sorry
   112→
   113→end Erdos501
   114→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 502
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/502](https://www.erdosproblems.com/502)
    23→
    24→Erdős asked (after Coxeter): what is the maximum size of a subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$
    25→with only two distinct pairwise distances? Bannai, Bannai, and Stanton proved an
    26→upper bound of $\binom{n+2}{2}$.
    27→
    28→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems_. Magyar Tud. Akad. Mat. Kutató Int. Közl. **6**
    29→(1961), 221–254. (p. 244)
    30→
    31→[BBS83] Bannai, E., Bannai, E., and Stanton, D., _An upper bound for the cardinality of an
    32→$s$-distance subset in real Euclidean space. II_. Combinatorica (1983), 147–152.
    33→
    34→[PePo21] Petrov, F. and Pohoata, C., _A remark on sets with few distances in $\mathbb{R}^d$_.
    35→Proc. Amer. Math. Soc. (2021), 569–571.
    36→
    37→See also Problem 1089 for the general $s$-distance version of this question.
    38→
    39→OEIS: [A027627](https://oeis.org/A027627)
    40→-/
    41→
    42→namespace Erdos502
    43→
    44→/--
    45→A finite set of points in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is a two-distance set if there are exactly
    46→two distinct positive real numbers that occur as distances between pairs
    47→of distinct points. (Positivity is automatic since `dist x y > 0` for `x ≠ y`
    48→in Euclidean space.)
    49→-/
    50→noncomputable def IsTwoDistanceSet {n : ℕ} (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))) : Prop :=
    51→  Set.ncard {d : ℝ | ∃ x ∈ A, ∃ y ∈ A, x ≠ y ∧ d = dist x y} = 2
    52→
    53→/--
    54→Erdős Problem 502 — Bannai–Bannai–Stanton Upper Bound [Er61] [BBS83]:
    55→
    56→Any two-distance set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ has at most $\binom{n+2}{2}$ elements.
    57→A simpler proof was given by Petrov and Pohoata [PePo21]. The best known lower bound
    58→is $\binom{n+1}{2}$ via a construction of Alweiss.
    59→-/
    60→@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
    61→theorem erdos_502
    62→    (n : ℕ) (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)))
    63→    (hA : IsTwoDistanceSet A) :
    64→    A.card ≤ Nat.choose (n + 2) 2 := by
    65→  sorry
    66→
    67→/--
    68→Erdős Problem 502 — Lower Bound (Alweiss construction):
    69→
    70→There exists a two-distance set in $\mathbb{R}^n$ of size $\binom{n+1}{2}$.
    71→This is the best known lower bound, complementing the Bannai–Bannai–Stanton
    72→upper bound of $\binom{n+2}{2}$. See also Problem 503.
    73→-/
    74→@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
    75→theorem erdos_502_lower
    76→    (n : ℕ) :
    77→    ∃ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)),
    78→      IsTwoDistanceSet A ∧ A.card = Nat.choose (n + 1) 2 := by
    79→  sorry
    80→
    81→end Erdos502
    82→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 504
    21→
    22→Determine the maximum guaranteed angle α_N for N-point planar sets: the supremum of all
    23→angles α such that every set of N points in the plane contains three distinct points forming
    24→an angle of at least α.
    25→
    26→*References:*
    27→- [erdosproblems.com/504](https://www.erdosproblems.com/504)
    28→- [Sz41] Szekeres, Gy., _On an extremum problem in the plane_. American Journal of
    29→  Mathematics **63** (1941), 208–210.
    30→- [ErSz60] Erdős, P., Szekeres, G., _On some extremum problems in elementary geometry_.
    31→  Annales Universitatis Scientiarum Budapestinensis de Rolando Eötvös Nominatae, Sectio
    32→  Mathematica **3** (1960/61), 53–62.
    33→- [Se92] Sendov, Bl., _On a conjecture of P. Erdős and G. Szekeres_. Comptes Rendus de
    34→  l'Académie Bulgare des Sciences **45** (1992), 17–20.
    35→- [Se93] Sendov, Bl., _Angles in a plane configuration of points_. Comptes Rendus de
    36→  l'Académie Bulgare des Sciences **46** (1993), 27–30.
    37→-/
    38→
    39→open scoped EuclideanGeometry
    40→
    41→namespace Erdos504
    42→
    43→/--
    44→$\alpha_N$: the maximum guaranteed angle for $N$-point planar sets.
    45→The supremum of all $\alpha \in [0, \pi]$ such that every set of $N$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$
    46→contains three distinct points $x$, $y$, $z$ with angle at $y$ at least $\alpha$.
    47→-/
    48→noncomputable def maxGuaranteedAngle (N : ℕ) : ℝ :=
    49→  sSup {α : ℝ | 0 ≤ α ∧ α ≤ Real.pi ∧
    50→    ∀ (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))),
    51→      A.card = N →
    52→      ∃ x ∈ A, ∃ y ∈ A, ∃ z ∈ A,
    53→        x ≠ y ∧ y ≠ z ∧ x ≠ z ∧
    54→        ∠ x y z ≥ α}
    55→
    56→/--
    57→Erdős Problem 504 (Blumenthal's problem, solved by Sendov):
    58→
    59→Let $\alpha_N$ be the supremum of all $0 \leq \alpha \leq \pi$ such that in every set
    60→$A \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ of size $N$ there exist three distinct points $x, y, z \in A$
    61→such that the angle at $y$ (between rays $yx$ and $yz$) is at least $\alpha$.
    62→Determine $\alpha_N$.
    63→
    64→Sendov [Se93] proved that for $n \geq 3$ and $2^{n-1} < N \leq 2^n$:
    65→$$\alpha_N = \pi(1 - 1/n) \quad \text{when } N > 2^{n-1} + 2^{n-3}$$
    66→$$\alpha_N = \pi(1 - 1/(2n-1)) \quad \text{when } N \leq 2^{n-1} + 2^{n-3}$$
    67→-/
    68→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    69→theorem erdos_504 :
    70→    ∀ (N : ℕ), 4 < N →
    71→    ∀ (n : ℕ), 3 ≤ n → 2 ^ (n - 1) < N → N ≤ 2 ^ n →
    72→      maxGuaranteedAngle N =
    73→        answer(if 2 ^ (n - 1) + 2 ^ (n - 3) < N
    74→          then Real.pi * (1 - 1 / (n : ℝ))
    75→          else Real.pi * (1 - 1 / (2 * (n : ℝ) - 1))) := by
    76→  sorry
    77→
    78→end Erdos504
    79→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 505
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/505](https://www.erdosproblems.com/505)
    23→
    24→Borsuk's conjecture. Originally conjectured by Borsuk in 1933. Disproved by Kahn and Kalai
    25→[KaKa93] for $n > 2014$. The current smallest known counterexample dimension is $n = 64$, due to
    26→Brouwer and Jenrich [BrJe14]. Eggleston [Eg55] proved the conjecture for $n = 3$. Schramm [Sc88]
    27→proved the upper bound $\alpha(n) \leq ((3/2)^{1/2} + o(1))^n$ on the minimum number of pieces
    28→needed. Erdős [Er81b, p.28] suspected the conjecture was false for sufficiently high dimensions.
    29→
    30→[Er46b] Erdős, P., _On sets of distances of $n$ points_. Amer. Math. Monthly (1946), 248-250.
    31→
    32→[Er81b] Erdős, P., _My Scottish Book 'Problems'_. The Scottish Book (1981), 27-35.
    33→
    34→[Eg55] Eggleston, H. G., _Covering a three-dimensional set with sets of smaller diameter_.
    35→J. London Math. Soc. (1955), 11-24.
    36→
    37→[KaKa93] Kahn, J., Kalai, G., _A counterexample to Borsuk's conjecture_. Bull. Amer. Math. Soc.
    38→(N.S.) (1993), 60-62.
    39→
    40→[BrJe14] Brouwer, A. E., Jenrich, T., _A 64-dimensional counterexample to Borsuk's conjecture_.
    41→Electron. J. Combin. (2014), Paper 4.29, 3.
    42→
    43→[Sc88] Schramm, O., _Illuminating sets of constant width_. Mathematika (1988), 180-189.
    44→-/
    45→
    46→open Metric Set
    47→
    48→namespace Erdos505
    49→
    50→/--
    51→Borsuk's conjecture:
    52→Is every set of diameter $1$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ the union of at most $n+1$ sets of diameter
    53→$< 1$?
    54→
    55→This is trivially true for $n=1$ and easy for $n=2$. Eggleston proved it for $n=3$. In 1981,
    56→Erdős wrote that he suspected it was false for sufficiently high dimensions. Indeed, Kahn and
    57→Kalai disproved it for $n > 2014$. The current smallest known counterexample dimension is
    58→$n = 64$, due to Brouwer and Jenrich.
    59→-/
    60→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    61→theorem erdos_505 : answer(False) ↔
    62→    ∀ n : ℕ, ∀ S : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)),
    63→      Metric.diam S = 1 →
    64→      ∃ F : Fin (n + 1) → Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)),
    65→        S = ⋃ i, F i ∧ ∀ i, Metric.diam (F i) < 1 := by
    66→  sorry
    67→
    68→end Erdos505
    69→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→import FormalConjecturesForMathlib.Geometry.«2d»
    19→
    20→/-!
    21→# Erdős Problem 506
    22→
    23→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/506](https://www.erdosproblems.com/506)
    24→
    25→What is the minimum number of circles determined by $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$,
    26→not all on a circle and not all on a line?
    27→
    28→Related problems: [#104](https://www.erdosproblems.com/104),
    29→[#831](https://www.erdosproblems.com/831).
    30→
    31→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems_, 1961, p. 245.
    32→
    33→[El67] Elliott, P. D. T. A., _On the number of circles determined by $n$ points_,
    34→Acta Mathematica Academiae Scientiarum Hungaricae (1967), 181–188.
    35→
    36→[PuSm] Purdy, G. and Smith, J. W., _Lines, circles, and the number of determined circles_.
    37→
    38→[BaBa94] Bálintová, A. and Bálint, V., _On the number of circles determined by $n$ points
    39→in the Euclidean plane_, Acta Mathematica Hungarica (1994), 283–289.
    40→-/
    41→
    42→open Finset EuclideanGeometry
    43→
    44→namespace Erdos506
    45→
    46→/-- All points in $S$ lie on a common circle with positive radius. -/
    47→noncomputable def AllOnCircle (S : Finset ℝ²) : Prop :=
    48→  ∃ c : ℝ², ∃ r : ℝ, 0 < r ∧ ∀ p ∈ S, dist p c = r
    49→
    50→/-- The number of distinct circles determined by $S$. A circle is identified by its
    51→center and positive radius. It is "determined" by $S$ if at least $3$ points of $S$
    52→lie on it. -/
    53→noncomputable def numDeterminedCircles (S : Finset ℝ²) : ℕ :=
    54→  Set.ncard {p : ℝ² × ℝ |
    55→    0 < p.2 ∧ 3 ≤ Set.ncard {q ∈ (↑S : Set ℝ²) | dist q p.1 = p.2}}
    56→
    57→/-- The minimum number of circles determined by any configuration of $n$ points in
    58→$\mathbb{R}^2$ that are neither all on a circle nor all collinear. Returns $0$ when
    59→no such configuration exists. -/
    60→noncomputable def minDeterminedCircles (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    61→  sInf (numDeterminedCircles '' {S : Finset ℝ² |
    62→    S.card = n ∧ ¬AllOnCircle S ∧ ¬Collinear ℝ (↑S : Set ℝ²)})
    63→
    64→/--
    65→Erdős Problem 506 [Er61, p. 245]:
    66→
    67→What is the minimum number of circles determined by $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, not all on a
    68→circle (and not all on a line)?
    69→
    70→Elliott [El67] proved that for $n > 393$ points not all on a circle or a line, the
    71→points determine at least $\binom{n-1}{2}$ distinct circles. Purdy and Smith [PuSm]
    72→corrected this to the sharper bound $\binom{n-1}{2} + 1 - \lfloor(n-1)/2\rfloor$, which is best
    73→possible (witnessed by $n-1$ points on a circle and one point off it).
    74→-/
    75→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    76→theorem erdos_506 : ∀ n : ℕ, 393 < n →
    77→    minDeterminedCircles n =
    78→      answer(Nat.choose (n - 1) 2 + 1 - (n - 1) / 2) := by
    79→  sorry
    80→
    81→end Erdos506
    82→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 511
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/511](https://www.erdosproblems.com/511)
    23→
    24→Let $f(z) \in \mathbb{C}[z]$ be a monic polynomial of degree $n$. Is it true that,
    25→for every $c > 1$, the set $\{z \in \mathbb{C} : |f(z)| < 1\}$ has at most $O_c(1)$
    26→many connected components of diameter $> c$ (where the implied constant is
    27→independent of $n$)?
    28→
    29→Disproved by Pommerenke [Po61], who showed that for any $0 < d < 4$ and $k \geq 1$,
    30→there exist monic polynomials $f \in \mathbb{C}[x]$ such that
    31→$\{z : |f(z)| \leq 1\}$ has at least $k$ connected components of diameter $\geq d$.
    32→Huang [Hu25] independently obtained the same result.
    33→
    34→Pólya [Po28] proved that no connected component of $\{z : |f(z)| \leq 1\}$ can have
    35→diameter exceeding $4$, so the conjecture is vacuously true for $c \geq 4$.
    36→
    37→[EHP58] Erdős, P., Herzog, F., and Piranian, G., *Metric properties of polynomials*,
    38→J. Analyse Math. 6 (1958), 125–148.
    39→
    40→[Er61] Erdős, P., *Some unsolved problems*, Magyar Tud. Akad. Mat. Kutató Int. Közl.
    41→6 (1961), 221–254.
    42→
    43→[Ha74] Hayman, W. K., *Research problems in function theory: new problems*, (1974),
    44→155–180.
    45→
    46→[Po28] Pólya, G., *Beitrag zur Verallgemeinerung des Verzerrungssatzes auf mehrfach
    47→zusammenhängende Gebiete*, S.-B. Preuss. Akad. Wiss. (1928), 228–232, 280–282.
    48→
    49→[Po61] Pommerenke, Ch., *On metric properties of complex polynomials*, Michigan Math. J.
    50→8 (1961), 97–115.
    51→
    52→[Hu25] Huang, L., *Many lemniscates with large diameter*, arXiv:2509.11597, 2025.
    53→-/
    54→
    55→open Polynomial Set
    56→
    57→namespace Erdos511
    58→
    59→/-- Erdős Problem 511: Is it true that for every $c > 1$, there exists a bound $M$
    60→(independent of the degree) such that for any monic polynomial $f \in \mathbb{C}[z]$,
    61→the sublevel set $\{z \in \mathbb{C} : \|f(z)\| < 1\}$ has at most $M$ connected
    62→components of diameter $> c$?
    63→
    64→Disproved by Pommerenke. -/
    65→@[category research solved, AMS 30]
    66→theorem erdos_511 : answer(False) ↔
    67→    ∀ c : ℝ, c > 1 →
    68→      ∃ M : ℕ, ∀ (f : Polynomial ℂ),
    69→        f.Monic →
    70→          ∀ (k : ℕ) (C : Fin k → Set ℂ),
    71→            (∀ i, (C i).Nonempty) →
    72→            (∀ i, C i ⊆ {z : ℂ | ‖Polynomial.eval z f‖ < 1}) →
    73→            (∀ i, IsPreconnected (C i)) →
    74→            (∀ i, Metric.diam (C i) > c) →
    75→            (∀ i j, i ≠ j → Disjoint (C i) (C j)) →
    76→            k ≤ M := by
    77→  sorry
    78→
    79→/-- Pólya's theorem [Po28]: Every connected component of the sublevel set
    80→$\{z \in \mathbb{C} : \|f(z)\| \leq 1\}$ for a monic polynomial $f$ has diameter at most $4$.
    81→This provides the complementary upper bound that explains why Problem 511 concerns $c > 1$:
    82→for $c \geq 4$, the conjecture is vacuously true. -/
    83→@[category research solved, AMS 30]
    84→theorem erdos_511_polya_upper :
    85→    ∀ (f : Polynomial ℂ), f.Monic →
    86→      ∀ (S : Set ℂ), S ⊆ {z : ℂ | ‖Polynomial.eval z f‖ ≤ 1} →
    87→        IsPreconnected S →
    88→          Metric.diam S ≤ 4 := by
    89→  sorry
    90→
    91→end Erdos511
    92→

    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 512
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/512](https://www.erdosproblems.com/512)
    23→
    24→This is Littlewood's conjecture, proved independently by Konyagin [Ko81]
    25→and McGehee, Pigno, and Smith [MPS81].
    26→
    27→[Ko81] Konyagin, S.V., _On a problem of Littlewood_, Izv. Akad. Nauk SSSR Ser. Mat.
    28→45 (1981), 243–265.
    29→
    30→[MPS81] McGehee, O.C., Pigno, L., and Smith, B., _Hardy's inequality and the $L^1$
    31→norm of exponential sums_, Ann. of Math. 113 (1981), 613–618.
    32→
    33→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems_. Magyar Tud. Akad. Mat. Kutató Int. Közl.
    34→6 (1961), 221–254.
    35→-/
    36→
    37→open Finset BigOperators MeasureTheory
    38→
    39→namespace Erdos512
    40→
    41→/-- The exponential sum $\sum_{n \in A} e(n\theta)$ where $e(x) = e^{2\pi ix}$. -/
    42→noncomputable def expSum512 (A : Finset ℤ) (θ : ℝ) : ℂ :=
    43→  ∑ n ∈ A, Complex.exp (2 * ↑Real.pi * ↑n * ↑θ * Complex.I)
    44→
    45→/-- Erdős Problem 512 (Littlewood's conjecture [Ko81] [MPS81]): There exists an absolute
    46→constant $C > 0$ such that for every finite set $A \subset \mathbb{Z}$ with $|A| \geq 2$,
    47→$$\int_0^1 \left| \sum_{n \in A} e(n\theta) \right| d\theta \geq C \cdot \log |A|.$$ -/
    48→@[category research solved, AMS 42]
    49→theorem erdos_512 :
    50→    ∃ C : ℝ, 0 < C ∧
    51→      ∀ A : Finset ℤ, 2 ≤ A.card →
    52→        C * Real.log (A.card : ℝ) ≤
    53→          ∫ θ in (0 : ℝ)..1, ‖expSum512 A θ‖ := by
    54→  sorry
    55→
    56→end Erdos512
    57→

1→/-
     2→Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.

     7→
     8→    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     9→
    10→Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→import Mathlib.Analysis.BoundedVariation
    19→
    20→/-!
    21→# Erdős Problem 514
    22→
    23→Does every entire transcendental function admit a continuous path going to infinity along which
    24→the function grows faster than any polynomial? A related question asks whether the growth rate
    25→along such a path can be estimated in terms of the maximum modulus.
    26→
    27→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/514](https://www.erdosproblems.com/514)
    28→
    29→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems_. Magyar Tud. Akad. Mat. Kutató Int. Közl. 6 (1961),
    30→p. 249.
    31→
    32→[Er82e] Erdős, P., _Problems and results on finite and infinite combinatorial analysis II_,
    33→L'Enseignement Math. 27 (1982), 163–176.
    34→-/
    35→
    36→open Complex Filter Topology Set
    37→
    38→namespace Erdos514
    39→
    40→/-- The maximum modulus of $f$ on the circle of radius $r$:
    41→$M(r) = \sup \{ \|f(z)\| \mid \|z\| = r \}$. -/
    42→noncomputable def maxModulus (f : ℂ → ℂ) (r : ℝ) : ℝ :=
    43→  sSup {x : ℝ | ∃ z : ℂ, ‖z‖ = r ∧ x = ‖f z‖}
    44→
    45→/-- Erdős Problem 514 (proved by Boas, unpublished):
    46→For every entire transcendental function $f$, there exists a continuous path $\gamma$
    47→going to infinity such that $|f(\gamma(t))/\gamma(t)^n| \to \infty$ for every $n$. -/
    48→@[category research solved, AMS 30]
    49→theorem erdos_514 :
    50→    ∀ f : ℂ → ℂ, Differentiable ℂ f →
    51→      (¬ ∃ p : Polynomial ℂ, ∀ z, f z = p.eval z) →
    52→      ∃ γ : ℝ → ℂ, Continuous γ ∧
    53→        Tendsto (fun t => ‖γ t‖) atTop atTop ∧
    54→        ∀ n : ℕ, Tendsto (fun t => ‖f (γ t)‖ / ‖γ t‖ ^ n) atTop atTop := by
    55→  sorry
    56→
    57→/-- Erdős Problem 514, growth rate variant (open):
    58→For every entire transcendental function $f$ and every $\varepsilon \in (0, 1)$, does there exist a
    59→continuous path $\gamma$ going to infinity along which $|f(z)| \geq M(|z|)^\varepsilon$? -/
    60→@[category research open, AMS 30]
    61→theorem erdos_514.variants.max_modulus_growth :
    62→    answer(sorry) ↔
    63→      ∀ f : ℂ → ℂ, Differentiable ℂ f →
    64→        (¬ ∃ p : Polynomial ℂ, ∀ z, f z = p.eval z) →
    65→        ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε → ε < 1 →
    66→          ∃ γ : ℝ → ℂ, Continuous γ ∧
    67→            Tendsto (fun t => ‖γ t‖) atTop atTop ∧
    68→            ∀ᶠ t in atTop, (maxModulus f ‖γ t‖) ^ ε ≤ ‖f (γ t)‖ := by
    69→  sorry
    70→
    71→/-- Erdős Problem 514, path length variant (open):
    72→Can the length of a path $\gamma$ to infinity (along which an entire transcendental function
    73→grows faster than any polynomial) be estimated in terms of the maximum modulus
    74→$M(r) = \sup_{|z|=r} |f(z)|$? More precisely, does there exist a function $g$ such that
    75→the arc length of $\gamma$ restricted to the disk of radius $R$ is at most $g(M(R))$? -/
    76→@[category research open, AMS 30]
    77→theorem erdos_514.variants.path_length :
    78→    answer(sorry) ↔
    79→      ∀ f : ℂ → ℂ, Differentiable ℂ f →
    80→        (¬ ∃ p : Polynomial ℂ, ∀ z, f z = p.eval z) →
    81→        ∃ γ : ℝ → ℂ, Continuous γ ∧
    82→          Tendsto (fun t => ‖γ t‖) atTop atTop ∧
    83→          (∀ n : ℕ, Tendsto (fun t => ‖f (γ t)‖ / ‖γ t‖ ^ n) atTop atTop) ∧
    84→          ∃ g : ℝ → ℝ, ∀ R : ℝ, 0 < R →
    85→            eVariationOn γ {t | ‖γ t‖ ≤ R} ≤ ENNReal.ofReal (g (maxModulus f R)) := by
    86→  sorry
    87→
    88→end Erdos514
    89→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 515
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/515](https://www.erdosproblems.com/515)
    23→
    24→Let $f(z)$ be an entire function, not a polynomial. Does there exist a locally
    25→rectifiable path $C$ tending to infinity such that, for every $\lambda > 0$, the integral
    26→$\int_C |f(z)|^{-\lambda} \, |dz|$ is finite?
    27→
    28→Huber [Hu57] proved the weaker result that for *each* $\lambda > 0$ there exists a
    29→path $C_\lambda$ with finite integral, but the path may depend on $\lambda$. The full
    30→result — a *single* path that works for all $\lambda > 0$ simultaneously — was proved
    31→in the affirmative. The case when $f$ has finite order was proved by Zhang [Zh77].
    32→The general case was proved by Lewis, Rossi, and Weitsman [LRW84], who in fact
    33→proved this with $|f|$ replaced by $e^u$ where $u$ is any subharmonic function.
    34→
    35→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems_. Magyar Tud. Akad. Mat. Kutató Int. Közl. 6 (1961),
    36→p. 249.
    37→
    38→[Er82e] Erdős, P., _Problems and results on finite and infinite combinatorial analysis II_,
    39→L'Enseignement Math. 27 (1982), 163–176.
    40→
    41→[Hu57] Huber, A., _On subharmonic functions and differential geometry in the large_. Comment.
    42→Math. Helv. 32 (1957), 13–72.
    43→
    44→[Zh77] Zhang, G., _Asymptotic values of entire and meromorphic functions_. Kexue Tongbao
    45→(1977), 480, 486.
    46→
    47→[LRW84] Lewis, J., Rossi, J. and Weitsman, A., _On the growth of subharmonic
    48→functions along paths_. Ark. Mat. 22 (1984), 109–119.
    49→-/
    50→
    51→open MeasureTheory Filter Set
    52→
    53→namespace Erdos515
    54→
    55→/--
    56→Erdős Problem 515: For any entire function $f$ that is not a polynomial, there
    57→exists a locally rectifiable path $\gamma$ tending to infinity such that for every
    58→$\lambda > 0$, the line integral $\int_\gamma |f(z)|^{-\lambda} \, |dz|$ is finite.
    59→
    60→The path $\gamma : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{C}$ is parametrized on $[0, \infty)$, is continuous
    61→with locally bounded variation (i.e., locally rectifiable), and satisfies
    62→$\|\gamma(t)\| \to \infty$ as $t \to \infty$. The integral
    63→$\int_\gamma |f(z)|^{-\lambda} \, |dz|$ is expressed as
    64→$\int_0^\infty \|f(\gamma(t))\|^{-\lambda} \cdot \|\gamma'(t)\| \, dt$.
    65→
    66→The case when $f$ has finite order was proved by Zhang [Zh77]. The general case
    67→was proved by Lewis, Rossi, and Weitsman [LRW84].
    68→-/
    69→@[category research solved, AMS 30]
    70→theorem erdos_515 : answer(True) ↔
    71→    ∀ (f : ℂ → ℂ),
    72→      Differentiable ℂ f →
    73→      (¬ ∃ p : Polynomial ℂ, ∀ z, f z = p.eval z) →
    74→      ∃ (γ : ℝ → ℂ),
    75→        Continuous γ ∧
    76→        LocallyBoundedVariationOn γ (Ici 0) ∧
    77→        Tendsto (fun t => ‖γ t‖) atTop atTop ∧
    78→        ∀ l : ℝ, 0 < l →
    79→          IntegrableOn
    80→            (fun t => ‖f (γ t)‖ ^ (-l) * ‖deriv γ t‖)
    81→            (Ici 0)
    82→            volume := by
    83→  sorry
    84→
    85→end Erdos515
    86→

    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.

    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 518
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/518](https://www.erdosproblems.com/518)
    23→
    24→A problem of Erdős and Gyárfás [ErGy95]. Gerencsér and Gyárfás [GeGy67]
    25→proved that, if the paths do not need to be of the same colour, then two
    26→paths suffice. Erdős and Gyárfás proved that $2\sqrt{n}$ paths suffice and
    27→observed that $\sqrt{n}$ would be best possible.
    28→
    29→Solved in the affirmative by Pokrovskiy, Versteegen, and Williams [PVW24].
    30→
    31→[ErGy95] Erdős, P., Gyárfás, A., _Vertex covering with monochromatic paths_.
    32→Math. Pannon. (1995), 7-10.
    33→
    34→[GeGy67] Gerencsér, L., Gyárfás, A., _On Ramsey-type problems_.
    35→Ann. Univ. Sci. Budapest. Eötvös Sect. Math. (1967), 167-170.
    36→
    37→[PVW24] Pokrovskiy, A., Versteegen, L., Williams, E., _A proof of a conjecture
    38→of Erdős and Gyárfás on monochromatic path covers_. arXiv:2409.03623 (2024).
    39→-/
    40→
    41→namespace Erdos518
    42→
    43→/-- A path is monochromatic of color $b$ under edge coloring $c$ if every
    44→    consecutive pair of vertices in the path receives color $b$. A path
    45→    of length $\leq 1$ is trivially monochromatic. -/
    46→def IsMonochromaticPath {α : Type*} (c : α → α → Bool) (b : Bool) (p : List α) : Prop :=
    47→  p.IsChain (fun u v => c u v = b)
    48→
    49→/--
    50→Erdős Problem 518 [ErGy95]:
    51→
    52→Is it true that, in any two-colouring of the edges of $K_n$, there exist
    53→at most $\lceil\sqrt{n}\rceil$ vertex-disjoint monochromatic paths, all of
    54→the same colour, which together cover all vertices?
    55→
    56→Solved in the affirmative by Pokrovskiy, Versteegen, and Williams [PVW24].
    57→-/
    58→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    59→theorem erdos_518 : answer(True) ↔
    60→    ∀ (n : ℕ) (c : Fin n → Fin n → Bool),
    61→      (∀ i j : Fin n, c i j = c j i) →
    62→      ∃ (b : Bool) (paths : List (List (Fin n))),
    63→        paths.length ≤ ⌈Real.sqrt (↑n)⌉₊ ∧
    64→        paths.Pairwise List.Disjoint ∧
    65→        (∀ p ∈ paths, p.Nodup ∧ IsMonochromaticPath c b p) ∧
    66→        (∀ v : Fin n, ∃ p ∈ paths, v ∈ p) := by sorry
    67→
    68→end Erdos518
    69→
